definition: Derivation doubtful; conn. with nāṭya, mimic representation, dramatic art? In Nag. and Naw. it is written with dental t. Pigeaud has published and translated fragments of a text of this name. There we find: nawa ṅaran iṅ sasaṅa, natya ṅaran iṅ smita (5a). So perhaps: the nine modes of expression.

citations.text: 145.1: taṅeh yan ucapĕn kalaṅwan i hĕnū nira kadi nawanatya niṅ kawi;
91.8: nā ṅ nawanatya kapwa tinapak nira tinĕwĕkakĕn / hasya makādi tan pĕgat ikaṅ guyu ... / mwaṅ karuṇâmaṅun taṅis;

. 1a: iti nawanatya sawitā hulahakĕna: mṛga, matsya, pāna, dyuta, śṛṅgara, hasya, samara, śrama, kalaṅĕn. It seems that the Nawanatya text deals especially with the ``nine modes of expression'' of the courtier in his service of the king; elsewhere we find those of the poet (Sum) and of the dancer (Nag). It does not seem likely that in Sum. and Nag. the Nawanatya text is meant, or any particular text. The derivation from Skt. nati seems questionable (cf Pig.).
